author: @timothyford1534
text: I meant every word so I'm glad to hear it was well taken. Not all the videos pertain to me and the many things that I get into but I do enjoy them. replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I just thought the general level of videos on youtube lacked enough content to really follow through what people were demonstrating - i watched loads and always thought - 'why don't they tell me how they did it' - but not being one to complain about others i just thought - do it yourself if you think it is so important -so i did and people seem to like it

replies.author: @timothyford1534
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  Wow that's pretty awesome. I tend to ask questions from time to time about something specific from a video I've seen (usually twice) and I recently I have tried to complement a few that I see doing continuous good videos (good meaning that more than just a few people or select group inside an interest group or topic, can comprehend) but they are few. Mostly because I too am with you on the fact that there are too many people who have an exhibit on display through means of video but don't say enough on how they got to their final product or worse yet, those who say nothing at all nor post much if any at all of a written description of their master piece. Its one thing to be able to make something and not fully understand how or why it works past putting the pieces together. Those types usually try to explain something if only the process to create. I'm also not too wild about those who only say what their subject matter of final product does and just leaves it at that. Though who am I to judge too much anyhow. I have not yet produced a video. I know a decent amount about many things but I usually find an at least half decent video of the stuff I'm interested in on here already. More often than not clouded by many other less reputable reproductions or down right fakes. So I have avoided the malarkey of being "just another one of those" types. So until I find my unique niche like you and some others I admire, I'll just continue my quest to quench my thirst for knowledge and hope to run into other good people. Hey thanks for being down to earth and sending your replies. I'm sure that you are plenty busy and so I'll leave you alone now lol. Oh, and I hope that you and your Mrs. have a Merry Christmas this year.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Timothy Ford bless your heart mate and have a goo dxmas too

author: @timothyford1534
text: I was checking out the comments on one of your video's and seen your response to someone's comment complaining about an audio resolution dispute which upon clicking on the link you gave I watched this video. In your defense, that wasn't much of a rant. I personally want to Thank You Mr. Smith for all the great information that you share with us and make available for free. Looking at the number of subscribers you have being 14,000+ brings it home for me that you are truly an awesome person for keeping your position of wanting to make information available to everyone so that anyone can learn from each other. I loved the piece where you said that if you don't like the quality of the video's then the choice someone has is quite simple.. Lol that's too good. Okay sorry for the winded response here just wanted to say Thank You for all that you do.

author: @gsvjv
text:
Your videos are ABSOLUTELY FINE. Incredibly valuable information shared with a generosity of spirit that I personally find astounding. Anybody getting distracted by the "quality" of the presentation is missing the point totally and quite frankly they should learn to focus.
I, by the way, create graphics, audio and video as part of my daily routine for corporate clients, so it isn't as though I don't notice the imperfections and room for improvement, I just don't care about that.
I am here to get information which you so generously supply FOR FREE and am absolutely thankful and indebted for everything you have done.

author: @keeganmaloney3440
text:
I remember the early days with the bad audio and such, yet it was still worth listening too. The information presented here is fascinating and potentially very useful and thats why I watch them. 

When you are a content provider on the internet even if you do not charge, you will run into people complaining about this or that, but it really is just because so many people are coming in contact with your content that if 1% of them complain you will be inundated with them. I wouldn't worry about it. You know why your content is valuable.

I am recording a video on mnemonics next week to launch a new channel on youtube. I dont have a camera except my phone so I'm going to use graphics instead of video recording. I am also just using the recording function on my phone for the audio. We'll see how it turns out.

author: @Zerkbern
text:
Something I didn't know and took me a long time to discover:  while the video is running your comments are right there below the video.   As soon as the video completes, the comments are gone.

So anyone scrolling down after the end of the video doesn't see the comments.  They have to actively click on the ABOUT button to see them.

I know this may seem simple but it can be easy to miss.  Someone finally clued me  in.  You might mention from time to time that they need to click on ABOUT to see the comment information. 

Thanks for your work.  I don't need my information spit and polished.  You effort is appreciated.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I didn't know that either - i will mention it - thanks for the heads up mate

author: @joeglory425
text: I'd actually would be willing to pay to watch your channel.  I think the information is worth it.  The video quality sometimes but usually is not an issue.  There have been times I've been challenged to understand what was written on a white board (mostly I think because of glare rather than camera quality) and recent videos displaying voltmeter readings where I could see a number but now make out whether there were decimals or whether the readings mV or V.  That could be improved by just telling us in the video what the meter is reading.  I understand in moments of excitement this might not always happen!  Keep up the great work!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I will definitely do what you suggest and I think I will try and come up with something to reduce reverb - I don't think I am going to charge though but thanks for saying that

replies.author: @joeglory425
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I think the sound for videos done in your home is perfectly adequate.  The sound for your videos in your new shop could be improved.  I think the problem is more echo than reverb though the end result sounds about the same.  Your shop probably many more hard surfaces compared to your home.  Things like carpet absorb sound while tile floor reflects the sound.  In your shop the camera first receives sound directly from your mouth but then with varying delays receives it again from the walls, ceiling, and floor.  The end result sounds a lot like reverb.  The sound quality in your shop could probably be improved with minimal investment by mounting the camera in the middle of a piece of plywood to which a piece of carpet or acoustic foam or maybe even blankets have been applied.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @FredGandt
text:
Here here! :-)

Simple maths: 7000 people didn't subscribe for "Dumb people and flashing lights".
I was hooked by one of your early videos with the terrible sound and messy picture, but it was the content that hooked me.
Please keep doing what you're doing, *your way*.

Although, that said: There is a lot to be said for clarity, and the reverb in the lab is quite distracting. Not enough for a sharp mind, but dull minds need a helping hand.
To that end, perhaps some strategically placed egg boxes might help?
Really cheap (free from a friendly local veg shop). Environmentally _meh_ (damage is already done. Pretty effective for sound dampening.
